We mainly analyze how the model input question-answer records influence the model output, i.e., the predictions on learners� future performance. In particular, we consider the effects of both the skill information (i.e., the specific skill being tested) and temporal information (i.e., when the exercise was completed) on the model�s decision.We selected correctly-predicted sequences and set the skill and position of the last record in each sequence as the target skill and prediction position. Then each question-answer record in the sequence was tagged as either Same skillor Different skillbased on whether its skill matched the target skill, and Recent(i.e., the first half QA records) and Distant(i.e., the second half QA records) based on its distance from the prediction position. This resulted in four groups (i.e., 2 _ 2): Distant & Same skill, Distant & Different skill, Recent & Same skill, and Recent & Different skill. For each group, the absolute relevance of each question-answer record was summed to compute the mean. We computed and compared the mean of four groups among all correctly-predicted sequences across the five DLKT models in the ASSISTment2009 and ASSISTment2015 datasets.(a) ASSISTment2009 (b) ASSISTment2015Figure 4.Skill-distance relevance comparison for RNN-based DKT.(a) ASSISTment2009 (b) ASSISTment20157
The results, shown in Figure 4, 5, 6, 7, and 8, indicate that for all five DLKT models, the recent-same-skill records (i.e., records that are close to the prediction position and on the target skill) have the highest relevance to the prediction, while those distant-different-skillrecords (i.e., records that are far from the prediction position and on non-target skills) have the lowest relevance.

The paper addresses interpretability by critiquing existing DLKT methods for their opacity and lack of focus on the learning process. It introduces GRKT, which uses a graph-based approach and pedagogical theories to model the learning process in a more interpretable way, focusing on process-level interpretability. This is achieved through a three-stage modeling process that provides insights into how knowledge mastery evolves.

Specifically, when he or she answers a question correctly (incorrectly), his or her knowledge state of the corresponding latent concept increases (decreases). For example, he or she acquires knowledge of the concept �multiplication and division of fractions� after successfully completing the question in the third exercise step. Subsequent questions reveal a gradual grasp of this concept, contrasting with a lack of understanding of �factors and highest common factor.� Because he or she completed all the questions on �multiplication and division of fractions�, but skipped some related to �factors and highest common factor.� However, an inconsistency arises: in the third exercise step, despite answering the questions correctly, his or her mastery of �factors and highest common factor� decreased slightly. This discrepancy is due to the model�s inability to trace the student accurately, especially when there are limited exercise records at the beginning. Improved performance could be achieved if the student accumulates more exercise records over subsequent steps.
The paper addresses interpretability by using Grad-CAM, and t-SNE to analyze and visualize the model's attention mechanisms and embedding strategies.

The paper addresses interpretability by using temporal convolutional networks and graph attention networks to capture temporal features and relationships between learning resources, and by employing an Actor-Critic reinforcement learning method to simulate real educational scenarios. This approach aims to make personalized learning path recommendations more understandable and aligned with learners' goals and dynamic features. Interpretability is evaluated through experiments demonstrating the model's ability to adapt to learners' changing knowledge states and goals.
